section.title: Doing Good and Avoiding Divisions
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns to the believer's life in society: submission to authorities, gentleness toward all, and readiness for every good work. He grounds this in a stunning gospel remembrance, we too were once enslaved to sin, but God our Savior saved us by His mercy through the washing of regeneration, and closes by urging devotion to good and the rejection of divisive, pointless quarrels.
section.thesis: Because God saved us by His kindness and mercy, not by our righteous deeds, washing and renewing us by the Spirit and justifying us by grace, we are freed to live gently and do good in the world, avoiding the empty controversies and divisions that destroy.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to good citizenship and gentleness; the gospel ground, remembering what we were and the mercy that saved us through the washing of regeneration; and the charge to devote ourselves to good works while rejecting divisive people and worthless quarrels.

verses.text: Remind the believers to submit to rulers and authorities, to be obedient and ready for every good work,
to malign no one, and to be peaceable and gentle, showing full consideration to everyone.
For at one time we too were foolish, disobedient, misled, and enslaved to all sorts of desires and pleasures, living in malice and envy, being hated and hating one another.

units.label: But God our Savior saved us

verses.text: But when the {{chrestotes|kindness}} of God our Savior and His {{philanthropia|love for mankind}} appeared,
He saved us, not by the righteous deeds we had done, but according to His mercy, through the washing of {{palingenesia|new birth}} and {{anakainosis|renewal}} by the Holy Spirit.
This is the Spirit He poured out on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Savior,
so that, having been {{dikaioo|justified}} by His grace, we would become heirs with the hope of eternal life.

units.label: Devote yourself to good, avoid divisions

verses.text: This saying is trustworthy. And I want you to emphasize these things, so that those who have believed God will take care to devote themselves to good deeds. These things are excellent and profitable for the people.
But avoid foolish controversies, genealogies, arguments, and quarrels about the law, because these things are pointless and worthless.
Reject a {{hairetikos|divisive}} man after a first and second admonition,
knowing that such a man is corrupt and sinful; he is self-condemned.

chrestotes.gloss: Kindness, goodness, generous benevolence in action. It names the warm, gracious disposition of God our Saviour that appeared in Christ. Paired with His love for mankind, it stresses that salvation flows not from our merit but from the sheer kindness of God's heart toward undeserving people.

philanthropia.gloss: Love for mankind, benevolence toward humanity, the root of our word philanthropy. Applied to God, it describes His affectionate goodwill toward people. That this appeared, like the grace of chapter 2, points to the coming of Christ, in whom God's love for humanity took visible, saving form.

palingenesia.gloss: New birth, regeneration, a being born again. Used only twice in the New Testament, here of the believer's rebirth and in Matthew 19:28 of the renewal of all things. The washing of regeneration pictures the cleansing, life-giving work of the Spirit that makes a sinner new, as the BSB footnote notes, rendering it of regeneration.

anakainosis.gloss: Renewal, a making new again. It describes the Spirit's ongoing work of transforming the believer, closely joined here to the new birth. Together, washing, regeneration, and renewal portray salvation as a thorough inward renovation worked by the Holy Spirit, not a mere improvement of the old self.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare righteous. A key term of Paul's gospel: God counts the believer righteous by grace, on the basis of Christ, not their deeds. Here justification by His grace makes us heirs with the hope of eternal life, tying the legal verdict to the family inheritance.

hairetikos.gloss: Divisive, factious, given to causing splits, the root of our word heretic. It describes a person who stirs up parties and division in the church, often around the pointless controversies just mentioned. After patient warning, such a person is to be rejected, for their persistence exposes a corrupt and self-condemned heart.

basic.context: Paul tells Titus to remind believers how to live in the wider world: respecting authorities, doing good, speaking well of others, and being gentle to everyone. Then he explains why we can live this way: we were once trapped in sin ourselves, but God saved us out of pure kindness and mercy, washing and renewing us by His Spirit. He ends by urging devotion to good works and warning against people who cause needless division.
basic.hermeneutics: Verses 4-7 are one of the great gospel summaries in the Bible, spelling out that salvation is God's work by mercy, not ours by merit. The washing of regeneration describes the Spirit's cleansing, life-giving work; Christians differ on how it relates to baptism, but the emphasis is on God's saving act. The warnings about quarrels and divisive people are practical guardrails around a healthy church.
basic.originalLanguages: God's chrestotes, kindness, and His philanthropia, love for mankind, appeared in Christ. He saved us through the washing of palingenesia, new birth or regeneration, and anakainosis, renewal, by the Spirit. Having been dikaioo, justified, by grace, we became heirs. A divisive person, hairetikos, is to be rejected after warnings.
basic.typology: The washing of regeneration recalls the many Old Testament cleansings and the promise in Ezekiel that God would sprinkle clean water on His people and give them a new heart and His Spirit. Christ fulfils these shadows, cleansing and renewing His people inwardly.
basic.christ: God's kindness and love appeared in Jesus, our Saviour, echoing the appearing of grace in chapter 2. Through Him the Spirit is poured out abundantly, and by His grace we are justified and made heirs of eternal life. Salvation from first to last is centred on Christ and applied by the Spirit.
basic.god: God is our Saviour, kind and loving toward humanity, rich in mercy. He saves not because of our righteous deeds but because of His mercy, pouring out His Spirit generously and justifying us by grace. He is a generous God who makes enemies into heirs.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2 describes the same movement from dead in sin to saved by grace. Ezekiel 36 promises cleansing with water and a new heart by God's Spirit. And Romans 5 shows justification by grace leading to hope, just as here.
basic.oneStory: Here is the gospel in miniature: humanity enslaved to sin, then rescued purely by God's kindness and mercy, washed and renewed by the Spirit, justified by grace, and made heirs of eternal life. It is the same salvation promised through the prophets and secured in Christ, now producing a people who devote themselves to doing good in the world.

context.literary: The section moves from ethical exhortation, verses 1-2, to its gospel foundation, verses 3-7, marked by the sharp contrast of once we were, verse 3, and but when, verse 4. Verse 8 calls the gospel statement a trustworthy saying, one of several such formulas in the Pastorals, then applies it to good works and to avoiding division.
context.historical: Believers on Crete lived under Roman authority and among neighbours suspicious of new movements; Paul's call to submission and gentleness commends the faith and avoids needless offence. The foolish controversies and quarrels about the law reflect the Judaizing teachers of chapter 1, with their myths and genealogies.
context.canonical: Verses 4-7 rank with Ephesians 2:1-10 as a premier New Testament summary of salvation by grace. The trinitarian shape, the Father saving, the Spirit renewing, through Jesus Christ, makes it a treasured text on regeneration and justification.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To motivate godly, gentle living in society by rooting it in the gospel of God's unmerited mercy, and to protect the church's health by directing energy into good works rather than divisive, pointless disputes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The gospel of verses 4-7 describes what God has done and is foundational doctrine. The calls to submit to authorities, do good, and reject divisive people are prescriptive, though submission is not absolute, since Scripture elsewhere shows obedience to God takes precedence when rulers command sin.

debates.question: What is the washing of regeneration in verse 5?

views.label: The Spirit's inward cleansing and new birth
views.case: The washing pictures the Spirit's regenerating, cleansing work on the heart, fulfilling promises like Ezekiel 36:25-27, with the stress on God's saving act, not a rite.

views.label: A reference that includes baptism
views.case: Some link the washing to baptism as the outward sign of this inward renewal, while still grounding salvation in God's mercy and the Spirit, not the water itself.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: salvation is God's merciful, Spirit-wrought renewal, not a reward for our righteous deeds.

debates.question: How should the church treat a divisive person?

views.label: Patient warning, then rejection
views.case: Paul allows a first and second admonition, seeking repentance, before rejecting the person, showing both patience and a limit to it for the church's protection.

views.label: Protecting unity over indulging factions
views.case: The persistent factionalist is self-condemned; guarding the church's peace and truth eventually outweighs endless accommodation of the divisive.

debates.bearing: The passage models loving patience that still, in the end, acts decisively to protect the flock from those who will not stop dividing it.

study.originalLanguages: The turn from our old bondage to salvation hinges on two warm words for God: His chrestotes, kindness, and His philanthropia, love for mankind, both said to have appeared, echoing the appearing of grace in chapter 2. Salvation comes not by our righteous deeds but through the washing of palingenesia, new birth or regeneration, and anakainosis, renewal, by the Holy Spirit, a thorough inward renovation rather than mere self-improvement. Having been dikaioo, justified, declared righteous by grace, we become heirs of eternal life. Finally the hairetikos, the divisive, party-making person, once warned, is to be rejected, since persistence in division exposes a corrupt and self-condemned heart.

typology.type: The washing of regeneration and renewal by the Spirit
typology.fulfillment: Fulfils Ezekiel 36:25-27, God sprinkling clean water and giving a new heart and His Spirit, realised inwardly in the believer through Christ.
The inheritance promised to Abraham's children comes to all who are justified by grace in Christ, heirs of eternal life, Galatians 3:29 and 4:7.

study.christ: The kindness and love of God our Saviour appeared in Jesus, mirroring the appearing of grace in Titus 2:11. It is through Jesus Christ our Saviour that the Spirit is poured out on us abundantly, and by His grace that we are justified and made heirs of eternal life. Every strand of salvation here, kindness appearing, the Spirit given, justification granted, the inheritance secured, runs through Christ, so that the whole rescue from our old slavery to sin is His accomplishment applied by His Spirit.
study.god: God is our Saviour, and His character drives the passage: kindness, love for mankind, and mercy. He saves people who were foolish, disobedient, and enslaved, and He does it not because of any righteous deeds of theirs but purely out of mercy. He pours out His Spirit abundantly and justifies freely by grace, turning enemies into heirs. This is a lavishly generous God whose saving initiative is entirely His own.

crossReferences.note: The same move from enslaved in sin to saved by grace, not by works, purely by God's mercy and kindness.
God promises to sprinkle clean water, give a new heart, and put His Spirit within, fulfilled in the washing of regeneration.
Justified by faith, we have peace with God and rejoice in the hope of glory, paralleling justified by grace and heirs of hope.
The call to submit to governing authorities, echoed in the opening of this movement.

study.oneStory: This is the gospel in miniature and the whole Bible's storyline in brief: humanity lost and enslaved to sin, rescued not by merit but by the kindness and mercy of God our Saviour, cleansed and renewed by the Spirit, justified by grace, and made heirs of eternal life, exactly as the prophets foretold and Christ secured. Saved by such mercy, the church is freed to do good in the world and to guard its unity against pointless division, living out the new life it has received.

sermon.title: Jesus Christ Our Savior
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Titus, this message expounds Titus 3:4-6, the appearing of God's kindness and love and salvation through the washing of regeneration and renewal by the Spirit, the gospel heart of this movement. A clear, orthodox handling of the passage.

quiz.q: How does Paul say believers should live toward the wider world?

options.text: Submissive to authorities, ready for every good work, gentle and considerate to everyone.
options.note: Verses 1-2 call for good citizenship, good works, and gentleness.

options.text: Withdrawn and hostile toward outsiders.
options.note: The opposite; Paul urges peace, gentleness, and doing good to all.

options.text: Free to malign and quarrel with opponents.
options.note: Paul says to malign no one and to be peaceable.

options.text: Indifferent to how they are seen by others.
options.note: Their conduct is to commend the gospel before all.

quiz.q: What does Paul say we were like before God saved us?

options.text: Foolish, disobedient, misled, and enslaved to desires, living in malice and envy.
options.note: Verse 3 gives this sober portrait of our former state.

options.text: Basically good people who needed slight improvement.
options.note: Paul paints a picture of real bondage to sin, not minor faults.

options.text: Already righteous through our own efforts.
options.note: The point is that we were enslaved, not righteous.

options.text: Innocent victims with no sin of our own.
options.note: Paul names our own disobedience and malice, not mere victimhood.

quiz.q: On what basis did God save us, according to verse 5?

options.text: According to His mercy, not by the righteous deeds we had done.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly excludes our works and rests salvation on His mercy.

options.text: Because we had earned it by good behaviour.
options.note: Paul directly denies this; it is not by our righteous deeds.

options.text: Because we were more deserving than others.
options.note: Salvation is by mercy, ruling out any deserving on our part.

options.text: Because we first cleaned ourselves up.
options.note: It is God who washes and renews us, not we ourselves.

quiz.q: What is the result of being justified by His grace?

options.text: We become heirs with the hope of eternal life.
options.note: Verse 7 links justification to the inheritance and hope.

options.text: We earn a temporary probation.
options.note: The gift is a secure inheritance and hope, not probation.

options.text: We are left uncertain of our standing.
options.note: Justification gives a settled hope, not uncertainty.

options.text: We must now save ourselves by works.
options.note: Justification is by grace; good works flow from it, not for it.

quiz.q: How does Paul say the church should handle a divisive person?

options.text: Warn him once and twice, then reject him if he persists.
options.note: Verses 10-11 allow patient warning, then rejection of the unrepentant.

options.text: Ignore the division entirely.
options.note: Patience is shown, but persistent division must finally be confronted.

options.text: Reject him instantly with no warning.
options.note: Paul allows a first and second admonition before rejection.

options.text: Let him keep dividing the church indefinitely.
options.note: The divisive man is self-condemned and must not be indulged forever.

quiz.q: What does Paul want those who have believed God to devote themselves to?

options.text: Good works, which are excellent and profitable for everyone.
options.note: Verse 8 urges believers to devote themselves to good deeds.

options.text: Endless debates about genealogies and the law.
options.note: Paul calls those controversies pointless and worthless, verse 9.

options.text: Earning their salvation through effort.
options.note: Good works flow from grace received, not to earn salvation.

options.text: Private religion with no visible fruit.
options.note: The fruit is public, profitable good works.

questions: Paul says gentleness toward everyone flows from remembering what we once were. How does recalling your own rescue change the way you treat difficult or hostile people?
Salvation here is entirely God's merciful work, not our righteous deeds. Where are you still tempted to base your standing before God on your own performance?
God poured out His Spirit on us abundantly. In what area of life do you most need to lean on the Spirit's renewing work right now?
Believers are to devote themselves to good works that are profitable to others. What concrete good work is God prompting you toward in your community this week?
Paul warns against pouring energy into pointless controversies and divisions. Is there a quarrel you need to step away from so you can invest in what actually builds up the church?

translationNotes.note: Where the BSB prints an em dash before living in malice, house style renders it as a comma: desires and pleasures, living in malice and envy.
The washing of new birth translates the Greek for regeneration, as the BSB footnote notes; it pictures the Spirit's cleansing, life-giving renewal of the sinner.
This saying is trustworthy is one of the trustworthy sayings of the Pastoral Epistles, marking the preceding gospel summary as a settled, reliable confession.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Titus 3:1-11. The em dash in verse 3 has been rendered as a comma per house style.
